Amazon in the community: Here’s what’s happening in Seattle, Bellevue, and the Puget Sound

Learn how we invest in the community, help power the local economy, and support our employees in the Puget Sound region.

    Written by Amazon Staff

    Recent Updates
    June 5, 2026 3:12 PM

    Amazon unveils Whale Tail Selfless Station on Seattle campus

    Amazon Whale Tail selfless station unveiling. Andrew Devore (Amazon). Photo by Alabastro Photography.
    This summer, the world is coming to Seattle—and Amazon is welcoming them with public art rooted in the culture and community of the Pacific Northwest.
    On June 4, Amazon unveiled its Whale Tail Selfless Station on the Day 1 Playfield—one of 26 art pieces that are part of the Seattle Unity Loop. The Selfless Stations are large-scale public art and community engagement hubs where visitors can take photos, learn about local nonprofits, and donate to causes that matter.
    Amazon's whale tail was designed by Felicia "Felix" Bryan, an enrolled Chinook artist and scholarship recipient at Gage Academy of Art—one of Amazon's tenants on its Seattle campus. Raised in a family of artists, Felicia works across oil, beadwork, and block printing to tell stories of emotion, cultural heritage, and life experience. Their piece depicts the great blue heron (sbəq̓ʷaʔ), honoring Coast Salish spiritual traditions and the enduring connection between the natural and spiritual worlds.
    “To me, the blue heron represents not only a culturally significant bird, but a local icon found all along the Salish coast,” said Bryan. “Merging two iconic silhouettes together that, to me, represent Seattle the best, nature and culture, water and heritage.”
    The installation is part of Amazon's broader commitment to supporting Seattle communities this summer. Amazon is donating sports equipment to local organizations, sponsoring community games and clinics for families, and continuing to offer free public parking on evenings and weekends with free shuttle service to the stadium—making it easier for fans to get to games and encouraging the use of public transportation.
    The Whale Tail Selfless Station will remain on the Day 1 Playfield through the duration of the World Cup, before being moved to its permanent location on campus. Visitors are encouraged to stop by, take a photo, and learn more about the local nonprofits the program supports.

    Amazon to provide 2,000 free rapid response technology systems that restore critical services in minutes

    Each system is portable enough for one person to carry and set up, now scaling 50x ahead of hurricane season.

    June 4, 2026 1:40 PM

    Volunteers roll up their sleeves for spring cleanups across the Puget Sound

    During May’s Global Month of Volunteering, that meant rolling up their sleeves in two busy downtown neighborhoods: Seattle’s historic Pioneer Square and downtown Bellevue. Together, local volunteers helped care for shared public spaces and keep these neighborhoods clean and welcoming.

    Volunteers give Pioneer Square a spring refresh

    In Pioneer Square, Amazon supported the 16th annual Pioneer Square Spring Clean, where more than 200 volunteers gathered in Occidental Square before heading out across the neighborhood. Amazon volunteers joined community partners, including the Alliance for Pioneer Square and Seattle City Council members Dionne Foster and Alexis Mercedes Rinck, to pick up litter, plant, weed, mulch, and refresh one of Seattle’s most historic districts.
    The cleanup came at an important time for Pioneer Square. The neighborhood is home to many arts organizations, small businesses, and public spaces that bring people together before games, concerts, and the popular First Thursday Art Walk. As Seattle prepares to welcome visitors for the world's biggest soccer event, efforts like Spring Clean help support the neighborhood’s momentum and make sure the district is ready to welcome people from near and far.

    Amazon teams join the city of Bellevue for a downtown cleanup

    In Bellevue, more than 90 Amazon employees joined the city of Bellevue for a Keep Bellevue Beautiful cleanup. Volunteers began at The Meadow, Amazon’s 1.75-acre public space, before supporting litter cleanup and beautification efforts nearby. Beryl Tomay, Amazon’s vice president of transportation, joined Bellevue Mayor Mo Malakoutian for the event.
    Amazon’s Keep Bellevue Beautiful effort is part of a broader commitment to helping ensure Bellevue’s green spaces remain clean, accessible, and inviting.
    From downtown Seattle to Bellevue, these cleanups show how acts of service can make a visible difference in the communities Amazon employees and customers call home.

    7 ways Amazon’s global pro bono program is creating lasting community impact

    The 2025 Pro Bono Report showcases how Amazon teams are expanding our impact with innovative AI tools, professional expertise, and by partnering with organizations to create lasting impact in underserved communities.

    April 28, 2026 10:14 AM

    Amazon celebrates the opening of The Meadow at Bellevue 600

    Art installation by Oliver Jeffers called The Moon, the Earth and Us—two hand-painted sculptures of the Moon and Earth, scaled and positioned to reflect their true size and distance from one another.
    On April 27, Amazon celebrated the official opening of The Meadow—a new 1.75-acre public open space in the heart of downtown Bellevue at the company’s newest tower, the Bellevue 600 (B600) office development. Members of the Bellevue City Council, the city manager, city leadership staff, and community partners including the Bellevue Downtown Association joined Amazon for a ribbon-cutting ceremony.

    A space for art, nature, and community

    The Meadow is an open space built atop the future site of B600 Tower 2, designed to activate the site and welcome the community during interim development phases. The permanent portion of the space features an art installation by celebrated artist and children's book author Oliver Jeffers called The Moon, the Earth and Us—two hand-painted sculptures of the Moon and Earth, scaled and positioned to reflect their true size and distance from one another. Amazon acquired the piece last December and is giving it a permanent home in Bellevue. The urban meadow also features 100% native flowering plant species, designed to be a natural immersive garden with vibrant public spaces that is already providing habitat for native pollinators like birds and bees.

    Growing alongside Bellevue

    "Amazon's vision in Bellevue has always been to grow alongside this community," said Sean Lee, vice president of corporate real estate & facilities at Amazon. "Rather than closing off this site between development, we wanted to open it up—the Meadow gives the community a space to gather and enjoy right now. None of it is possible without the strong partnership we've built with the city of Bellevue and the community partners who help bring these spaces to life."
    Amazon has grown from 450 employees in Bellevue in 2017 to more than 15,000 today, making it the city's largest employer. B600 is Amazon's first self-developed project in Bellevue and a cornerstone of the Grand Connection. Amazon has also committed $22.6 million to local transportation projects and invested $100 million in affordable housing in Bellevue. Additionally, Amazon funds partnerships and projects across more than 80 organizations on the Eastside.

    Amazon commits over $70 million in scholarships to students across the US since 2019

    Amazon has awarded 200 high school seniors with scholarships of up to $40,000 each, plus a paid internship opportunity at Amazon.

    April 17, 2026 10:39 AM

    Amazon and Inspire Washington launch digital passport to drive arts engagement

    Photo by Seattle Theater group
    Building on momentum from the winter months, Amazon is continuing its partnership with Inspire Washington through “Discover What Moves You,” a campaign focused on increasing attendance at arts, heritage, and science experiences across the Puget Sound.
    The Discover What Moves You Digital Passport is an extension of the campaign—a free, mobile experience that launched March 23. The passport lets users explore participating cultural organizations and small businesses, unlock discounts, and earn points toward prizes through May 31.
    Participating organizations span the region, from interactive spaces like KidsQuest Children’s Museum and Gage Academy of Art, to performance venues including Theatre33, STG at Kerry Hall, and the Seattle Symphony at Benaroya Hall. Institutions like the Northwest African American Museum, National Nordic Museum, Kirkland Arts Center, and BelRed Arts Studio are also part of the experience.
    Participating partners like the Northwest African American Museum see the program as an opportunity to reach new audiences.
    “The Discover What Moves You program is a meaningful example of how partnerships can expand access to arts, culture, and shared experiences,” said Brandon Bird, president and chief executive officer of the Northwest African American Museum. “By connecting people to the organizations that bring our stories to life, this initiative helps ensure our cultural ecosystem remains accessible, relevant, and deeply rooted in the communities we serve.”
    The program is designed to reduce barriers to participation at local cultural institutions. It emerged from conversations with partners about fluctuating attendance patterns throughout the year—an opportunity to help people stay connected to the arts and culture organizations that are essential to the fabric of the Puget Sound. Working with Inspire Washington, Amazon aimed to make it easier for individuals and families to discover everything from free events to performances and exhibits, while supporting the organizations that make this region a special place to live.
    April 13, 2026 11:39 AM

    Amazon's $7.5 million investment helps transform a 122-year-old railroad bridge into a trail for everyone

    A 122-year-old wooden railroad bridge in Bellevue, Washington, is getting a new life—and a new purpose.
    The Wilburton Trestle, a 975-foot-long, 102-foot-tall structure originally built in 1904 for the Northern Pacific Railway, is being reimagined as a community asset that connects housing, jobs, and transit across the Eastside and transformed into a centerpiece of the Eastrail regional trail. When it opens in summer 2027, visitors will walk or roll along an 18-foot-wide trail deck with three viewing platforms offering panoramic views of the Bellevue skyline, Lake Washington, and the Olympic Mountains.
    Amazon is helping to make it happen. In September 2021, the company committed $7.5 million to the project—$5 million for the Wilburton Trestle and $2.5 million for the adjacent Wilburton Trail Segment—closing the funding gap needed to begin construction.
    "Amazon is proud to partner with King County on this iconic project, transforming a unique piece of our regional history into a permanent community asset for everyone," said David Zapolsky, Amazon's chief global affairs and legal officer. "Strong public-private partnerships make projects like the Wilburton Trestle possible, and make our region a great place to live, work, and visit. We can't wait for this part of Eastrail to open and serve generations to come."
    King County Executive Girmay Zahilay recently led a hard-hat tour of the trestle alongside Zapolsky, King County Councilmember Claudia Balducci, Bellevue Deputy Mayor Dave Hamilton, and other regional partners to mark the project's progress.
    Amazon's investment in the Wilburton Trestle reflects the company's commitment to the communities where its employees live and work. From preserving regional history to supporting multimodal transit infrastructure, projects like these help make the Puget Sound region a great place to call home for generations to come.

    Amazon plans to invest $25 billion in Mississippi data centers, create 2,000 jobs

    Amazon is creating high-paying jobs and strengthening the Magnolia State while building responsible infrastructure.

    March 28, 2026 9:04 AM

    Amazon celebrates opening of world's first floating bridge light rail connection

    Amazon celebrated a major milestone in regional transportation as the Crosslake Connection opened, bringing the world's first light rail system operating on a floating bridge to the Seattle-Bellevue area. The company served as presenting sponsor for the Transportation Choices Coalition's reception marking this historic achievement on March 27.

    Supporting sustainable transportation in the Puget Sound

    "We know that jobs and great workspaces matter, but so does how people get to them," said David Zapolsky, chief global affairs and legal officer at Amazon, highlighting the company's commitment to sustainable transportation infrastructure. "We are committed to ensuring our growth is sustainable and that we are investing in the right transportation infrastructure for this community. Public transit is core to our sustainability goals. It gives people reliable alternatives to driving, reduces congestion, and improves air quality for everyone."
    The new light rail connection is critical for the region's growing workforce. Amazon’s Puget Sound offices are strategically located within walking distance of transit stations, reinforcing the company's dedication to sustainable mobility solutions.

    $200 million commitment to transit-oriented affordable housing

    Amazon's transportation commitment extends beyond this celebration. The company has consistently supported regional transit efforts. In 2021, Amazon committed $100 million in partnership with Sound Transit to accelerate the creation of affordable housing units near light rail stations across the Puget Sound region. Last year, Amazon committed an additional $100 million to the City of Bellevue to accelerate affordable housing production, with a focus on transit-oriented sites that give low-to-moderate income families close proximity to community resources.
    This milestone reflects Amazon's broader philosophy of community partnership and long-term investment in the regions where it operates, demonstrating how public transit infrastructure supports both economic growth and environmental sustainability.

    Everything you need to know about Amazon’s housing fund—a $3.6 billion commitment to help people access affordable housing

    Amazon is innovating to help keep communities affordable, today and for generations to come.

    March 24, 2026 3:23 PM

    From Queen Anne to Othello: Amazon celebrates new affordable homes this week

    This week, Amazon joined partners across Seattle to celebrate two meaningful milestones in our ongoing effort to create and preserve affordable homes—a groundbreaking in Lower Queen Anne and a ribbon-cutting in the Othello neighborhood.

    Altaire at Queen Anne: Breaking ground on opportunity

    On March 9, the Urban League of Metropolitan Seattle and SRM broke ground on Altaire at Queen Anne, a 114-unit affordable rental housing development in Seattle's Lower Queen Anne neighborhood. The development will offer studio, one-, two-, and three-bedroom apartments for households earning between 40% and 70% of the area median income (AMI)—serving local workers, families, and artists in one of Seattle's higher-cost neighborhoods.
    For five years, the site sat vacant. Now, through a mission-driven public-private partnership—including more than $9 million from Amazon's housing fund alongside support from the Seattle Office of Housing, the Washington State Housing Finance Commission, Citibank, and PNC Bank—it will be transformed into a place people can call home.

    Atrium Court: A ribbon-cutting in Othello

    The very next day, on March 10, we celebrated the grand opening of Atrium Court—less than 200 feet from the Othello Light Rail Station. The development includes 200 income-restricted apartments affordable to households earning 50% to 80% of AMI.
    Amazon's housing fund contributed a $16 million below-market loan to help make this transit-oriented development a reality, alongside partners including Allivate Impact Capital, CEI-Boulos Capital Management, and Trez Capital.
    "Working families deserve the stability of knowing their home will remain affordable for generations to come," said Senthil Sankaran, managing principal, Amazon's housing fund. "Our partnership at Atrium Court highlights what's possible when mission-driven partners come together around a shared commitment: 99 years of affordability, right next to transit, in one of Seattle's most vibrant, diverse neighborhoods."
    This week, 385 more Seattle families moved one step closer to a stable, affordable home, all protected by our commitment to 99 years of affordability. That's what this work is all about.

    A guide to Amazon Literary Partnership grants—what to know and how to apply

    Applications are now open for 2026 grants supporting nonprofit literary organizations that empower writers across all genres.

    March 11, 2026 12:57 PM

    Celebrating Lunar New Year: Amazon joins communities across the Puget Sound

    This February, Amazon was proud to support Lunar New Year celebrations across the Puget Sound region—honoring traditions that bring families together and strengthen the cultural fabric of our communities.

    Partnering with Friends of Little Saigon for Tết

    From February 6-13, Amazon partnered with Friends of Little Saigon for their Tết Celebration, a series of cultural events for all ages. In Vietnamese culture, Tết symbolizes renewal, family, and cultural identity, offering neighbors a chance to experience Vietnamese traditions through food, art, and community connection.
    "Tết is a time to come together, honor our heritage, and welcome the new year with joy," said Quynh Pham, executive director of Friends of Little Saigon. "Partnering with Amazon allowed us to expand our celebration and welcome more neighbors into the heart of Vietnamese culture, helping make our region a place where everyone can participate in the traditions that make our communities whole."

    Celebrating with Seattle Symphony at Benaroya Hall

    On February 13, Amazon joined the Seattle Symphony Lunar New Year Gala at Benaroya Hall. This special evening brought together AAPI (Asian American and Pacific Islander) leaders and community members with music director Xian Zhang to celebrate the Year of the Horse through the power of music and shared cultural pride.

    Supporting family celebrations in Kirkland and Bellevue

    On February 28, Amazon supported two celebrations for Kirkland and Bellevue families. The City of Kirkland's celebration invited the community to explore traditional celebrations through food, crafts, dancing, and cultural demonstrations. That same day, the Emerald Parents Association, in partnership with Amazon, King County, and 4Culture, hosted its Lunar New Year celebration at Factoria Square Mall, featuring cultural exhibitions, a lion dance, food, crafts, games, and hanfu.
    "The exhibition created a vibrant, welcoming space for Chinese families in our region to celebrate Lunar New Year close to home—strengthening cultural identity, fostering pride, and deepening community connections," said Bev Ni, vice president of outreach at Emerald Parents Association.
    Amazon is grateful to help celebrate the traditions that make the Puget Sound region vibrant and welcoming for all.
    February 20, 2026 10:08 AM

    Amazon and USO Northwest team up to support over 2,000 deploying service members

    Amazon Volunteers pack hygiene and comfort kits, as 3,500 members of the military will be deployed to South Korea on 11 different flights throughout the beginning of 2026, at Amazon campus.
    This January, Amazon teamed up with USO Northwest to support 2,600 U.S. service members deploying to South Korea.
    Since 1941, the USO has supported service members and their families from enlistment through deployment and their transition home. Amazon has been a proud partner since 2022. USO Northwest operates across Washington, Oregon, and Idaho, serving over 150,000 military members and their families each year.

    Amazon volunteers assemble care packages for deploying service members

    On January 23, Seattle City Council member Bob Kettle joined more than 60 Amazon volunteers at our Seattle headquarters to assemble care packages and write encouraging letters for service members at Joint Base Lewis-McChord. The care packages will be distributed ahead of 11 flights to South Korea throughout March, providing comfort and connection to service members as they begin their overseas assignments.
    “Today, we’re grateful for partners like Amazon who help us carry out that mission by showing up in meaningful ways,” said Don Leingang, executive director for USO Northwest. “While it may look like a simple care package, gestures like these send a powerful message to service members: They are seen, valued, and supported by a grateful nation.”

    Warriors at Amazon leads military support efforts

    Leading the volunteer effort is the Warriors at Amazon, an employee-led group representing current and former military personnel, their families, and supporters. With more than 20,000 members, Warriors fosters an inclusive community for service members and veterans, helping them transition and thrive in civilian roles.
    This volunteer event reflects Amazon’s broader commitment to military families and service members. In 2024, Amazon surpassed our goal of hiring 100,000 veterans and military spouses, and our upskilling programs help veterans grow their careers. In the Puget Sound, Amazon supports organizations like the South Sound Military and Communities Partnership, Blue Star Families, and events like Seafair to celebrate those who serve.
    As thousands of service members head to South Korea, Amazon remains committed to supporting the military community in the Pacific Northwest and beyond.
    February 3, 2026 9:30 AM

    Affordable housing for longtime residents preserved in two Seattle neighborhoods

    The Brighton Apartments in South Seattle
    Two transformative affordable housing projects are moving forward in Seattle's historic neighborhoods, with support from Amazon's housing fund helping local developers preserve community stability and prevent displacement.

    Revitalizing the historic Eclipse Hotel in Chinatown-International District

    The historic Eclipse Hotel in Seattle Chinatown International District
    In Seattle's Chinatown-International District (CID), the Seattle Chinatown International District Preservation and Development Authority (SCIDpda) acquired and is rehabilitating the historic Eclipse Hotel. Built in 1908, this landmark building has stood at the heart of the community for over a century. While its ground-floor retail spaces remain vibrant and fully occupied, the upper floors have sat vacant since the 1970s. Through a $2 million Amazon grant, SCIDpda will convert these unused spaces into 39 affordable units—specifically designed for seniors earning at or below 40% of area median income (AMI). This mission-driven project ensures that longtime community members can age in the neighborhood they've called home for decades.

    Preserving affordability at Brighton Apartments in South Seattle

    In South Seattle, the Brighton Apartments—a 125-unit community built in 1968—is undergoing critical preservation work. Without intervention, the property faced rising rents and deteriorating conditions. With a $3.5 million grant from Amazon's housing fund, Brighton Communities (formerly known as the South Seattle Senior Foundation) can renovate while preserving affordability for 99 years. All homes will remain affordable for households earning between 30% and 80% of AMI.
    "Preserving affordable housing in neighborhoods like the CID and South Seattle ensures that the people who make our city work—first responders, teachers, health care workers—can continue to call these communities home," said David Zapolsky, Amazon chief global affairs and legal officer. "Working alongside mission-driven developers like SCIDpda and Brighton Communities, we're helping ensure our communities remain vibrant and accessible today and for generations to come."
    These projects demonstrate the power of partnership in preserving community stability and ensuring Seattle remains accessible to the service industry employees who make our city thrive.

    Amazon triples safety supplies at first wildfire relief hub in the US

    Amazon expands hub to help communities face the rising threat of wildfires in California and beyond.

    January 29, 2026 8:46 AM

    Amazon employee recognized as Kraken Hero of the Deep for pro bono work with Mary's Place

    Amazon attorney Yousri Omar was named a Seattle Kraken Hero of the Deep for leading legal clinics at Mary's Place for families experiencing homelessness. Omar serves on Mary's Place's board and helped build Amazon's Pro Bono Program. Amazon Legal has provided free legal services to Mary's Place for over 10 years, serving thousands of families.
    “I’m deeply honored to be recognized as a Hero of the Deep. This work is about showing up for families experiencing homelessness, and I’ve been privileged to do that alongside the incredible team at Mary’s Place,” said Omar. “I’m grateful to Amazon and my legal colleagues, whose commitment to pro bono service has made it possible to help families navigate some of their most difficult moments. This recognition really belongs to all of them.”

    Amazon's partnership with Mary's Place

    Since 2016, Amazon has donated over $100 million in cash and in-kind support to Mary's Place to address family homelessness in King County. In 2020, they opened a permanent shelter inside an Amazon building on the Seattle campus, serving 200 parents and children nightly and over 1,000 families annually.
    "Yousri and Amazon attorneys help parents navigate legal challenges that can make all the difference to secure stable housing,” said Dominique Alex, Mary’s Place chief executive officer. “We're so grateful to Yousri, every attorney who gives their time, and the Seattle Kraken for shining a light on this incredible partnership."
    Amazon employees also support Mary's Place through volunteer initiatives including donation organization, meal preparation, computer classes, and toy drives that have provided more than 5,000 toys for families.

    Recognition and community impact

    As part of the Hero of the Deep honor, Mary's Place will receive a $32,000 grant from the One Roof Foundation.
    This recognition reflects Amazon's commitment to supporting communities through employee expertise and partnerships that address critical needs like housing insecurity.

    Amazon supports Washington flood relief efforts with donations and supplies

    Amazon is donating $3 million and working with local first responders and front-line nonprofits to help communities that have been impacted by flooding in our hometown state.

    November 17, 2025 8:51 AM

    Honoring service and strength from Seattle

    From November 4-11, Amazon proudly joined Operation Green Light for Veterans—a nationwide effort led by the National Association of Counties (NACo) and the National Association of County Veterans Service Officers (NACVSO) to shine a green light in support of veterans, service members, and their families. Across the country, hundreds of Amazon buildings glowed green as a symbol of gratitude and unity.
    "By lighting hundreds of Amazon buildings green across the country for Operation Green Light, we're proud to stand alongside the National Association of Counties in honoring those who have served," said Charlotte La Belle, head of Amazon's global military affairs and U.S. Army veteran. "Veterans bring exceptional leadership, expertise, and dedication to Amazon every day. This initiative reflects our deep commitment to supporting their transition to roles in the private sector and encouraging other organizations to join us in celebrating and supporting our veteran community."
    Across the Puget Sound region, Amazon employees are turning their own lights green at home and volunteering with local veteran-focused organizations, creating a ripple of appreciation that extends beyond the workplace. The Warriors at Amazon employee-led group—representing more than 20,000 members globally—continues to lead by example, championing mentorship, service, and community engagement that strengthens Amazon's culture of belonging. Their dedication reminds us that honoring service isn't just about recognition, it's about action.
    As Amazon sites across Washington state joined in, Operation Green Light became more than a visual tribute—it became a reminder of Amazon's ongoing commitment to opportunity, belonging, and community for those who've served. Each act of service and each light, from Seattle to Spokane, illuminated what it means to stand united in gratitude.

    9 ways Amazon employees volunteered during our 2025 Global Month of Volunteering

    Here’s how Amazon employees support organizations and local communities around the world.

    November 6, 2025 8:59 AM

    Amazon expands its support for Puget Sound food banks with over $2.25 million in additional funding

    Amazon is dedicated to ensuring communities have reliable access to food year-round. As part of our overall commitment, we’ve donated an additional $2.25 million to local food banks in the Puget Sound region to meet increased demand. Throughout 2025, we’ve also used our transportation network to deliver more than 4.5 million meals, on behalf of local food banks, directly to Puget Sound residents and have donated more than 2.8 million meals to local community organizations to help those experiencing hunger in Washington state.
    "Food security remains a serious challenge in the Puget Sound region, with recent demand on local food banks reaching record levels," said Bettina Stix, global director of community impact at Amazon. "These donations combined with Amazon’s free home delivery and bulk delivery services offered to food banks strengthen the infrastructure of our local food system and ensure our neighbors have reliable access to nutritious meals."
    The contributions support key regional organizations addressing food insecurity including, Food Lifeline, Snoqualmie Food Bank, Edmonds Food Bank, Northwest Harvest, Emergency Food Network, Bellevue LifeSpring, Hopelink, and Renewal Food Bank. These funds will help provide thousands of families with nutritious meals, holiday food assistance, and year-round hunger relief services.
    Amazon helps food banks reach more people using our established last-mile infrastructure and logistics expertise. On top of financial donations, our delivery network—including Delivery Service Partners, Amazon Flex, and Amazon Shipping—has delivered more than 60 million meals directly to over 200,000 families experiencing food insecurity across the United States since 2020.
    We also provide bulk transportation services for free, moving tens of thousands of pounds of food weekly between suppliers, food banks, and community distribution points using our fleet of vehicles. In addition, our grocery stores regularly donate excess food donations to local food banks. Together, these efforts help ensure food reaches families in need regardless of transportation costs, barriers, or mobility challenges.
    Learn more about Amazon’s commitment to food security.

    How Amazon is helping flood-impacted communities in Mexico

    We are donating and delivering emergency supplies to impacted communities from our Disaster Relief hub in Mexico.

    October 29, 2025 9:00 AM

    Amazon and Gary Sinise Foundation donate vital equipment to Puget Sound area firefighters

    Amazon, in partnership with the Gary Sinise Foundation (GSF), donated more than $100,000 in essential equipment to Seattle-area firefighters in honor of National First Responders Day on October 28.
    The donation included a $56,000 grant to the Bellevue Fire Foundation for thermal imaging cameras that allow firefighters to see through smoke and darkness, and $50,000 to South King Fire Department in Federal Way for 14 self-contained breathing apparatuses and two night-vision helmets.
    During the events, Amazon volunteers and local public officials served meals to first responders to thank them for their service and dedication to community safety.
    "First responders play an essential role in keeping our communities safe, and we're proud to support the Gary Sinise Foundation to help ensure fire departments across Puget Sound have the equipment and resources they need," said Dean Allsopp, community engagement manager for Amazon.
    With this recent contribution, the total amount of first responder grants provided across Washington state now exceeds $450,000. Previous grantees include Spokane County Fire District 10, Franklin County Fire District, Everett Fire Department, West Pierce Fire and Rescue, and Seattle Fire Department.
    "Through our partnership, Amazon has displayed incredible compassion for our mission and a commitment to delivering direct meaningful impact to the heroes of our communities, the first responders who put their lives on the line every day to keep us safe," said Chrissy Kreisel, senior director of corporate and foundation relations at the Gary Sinise Foundation.
    The Gary Sinise Foundation was established in 2011 by actor and humanitarian Gary Sinise, who has supported veterans, first responders, and defenders for more than 40 years. Amazon's three-year partnership with GSF extends beyond Washington state, supporting programs including annual healing retreats for families of fallen heroes and the construction of specially adapted smart homes nationwide.

    4 ways Amazon is investing in New York

    Amazon's investments are boosting the Empire State's economy while creating opportunities for New Yorkers.

    September 30, 2025 3:34 PM

    Celebrating culture and community at Seattle's 18th annual Chinatown International District (CID) Night Market

    Last year, Amazon stepped up and supported the CID Business Improvement Area to bring the Night Market back to the historic neighborhood.
    Amazon proudly presented the 18th annual Chinatown-International District (CID) Night Market Saturday, September 26, marking the company's second consecutive year supporting the vibrant community celebration. During the event, the historic streets of Seattle's CID were transformed into a lively festival featuring more than 100 local vendors, performers, and thousands of neighbors and community members.
    "The Night Market has always held a special place in my heart. Growing up in the Chinatown-International District, I experienced firsthand how these cultural celebrations strengthen the fabric of our community," said Taylor Hoang, senior manager for Community Engagement at Amazon. "This isn't just about one day and one night of festivities, it's about honoring traditions, supporting family-owned businesses, and ensuring this historic neighborhood continues to thrive. That's what being a good neighbor truly means—showing up consistently and contributing meaningfully to the communities where we live and work."
    This year's Night Market overlapped with the Mid-Autumn Moon Festival, a 3,000-year-old tradition celebrated across Asia that marks the end of the summer harvest. The Seattle celebration made its debut in August 2006 as a small gathering organized by the youth leadership group WILD (Wilderness Inner-city Leadership Development) to bring residents, visitors, and vendors into the neighborhood as part of National Night Out. In 2023, the Night Market was canceled due to a lack of sponsorship. The following year, Amazon stepped in with a $250,000 three-year commitment as the lead sponsor.
    "The Night Market is a great event because it shows what the neighborhood could look like at its peak," said Tuyen Than, executive director of the Chinatown-International District Business Improvement Area. "And that's what we're going for: a Night Market, but all throughout the year."
    September 26, 2025 10:12 AM

    Amazon celebrates grand opening of Rainier Valley Food Bank's new facility

    The Rainier Valley Food Bank celebrated the grand opening of its newly renovated facility this month, welcoming hundreds of community members to the expanded space. Amazon contributed $1 million to the food bank's capital campaign, helping support its transformation from a small 1,200-square-foot location into a nearly 12,000-square-foot resource center.
    "Amazon's generous contribution to Rainier Valley Food Bank directly addresses one of our city's most pressing challenges—ensuring all Seattle residents have access to affordable, nutritious food," said Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell. "As we continue efforts to expand healthy food access, we recognize this investment doesn't just expand a building - it strengthens a vital community resource that provides dignity, nutrition, and hope for thousands of South Seattle residents."
    Amazon's contribution reflects the company's commitment to addressing food insecurity in its hometown communities. Using the same delivery network that serves our retail customers, Amazon partners with food banks like Rainier Valley Food Bank to bring groceries directly to the doorsteps of families in need for free. Since 2020, Amazon has delivered more than 50 million meals across the US and UK, including 4.5 million meals in the Puget Sound region alone.
    The renovation of Rainier Valley Food Bank not only expands its footprint, allowing for more space to help people in need, but it also creates an elevated shopping experience and offers services beyond only food distribution. This includes:
    A grocery-store-style shopping area with refrigerated sections, fresh produce, and essential items
    • A commercial kitchen for preparing ready-to-go lunches and hot meals
    • A dedicated resource center providing housing assistance and employment information
    • Outdoor courtyard and garden space for community gatherings
    The new facility is now open providing essential services to South Seattle residents in need. Learn more about Amazon's commitment to food security and how we're helping fight hunger in our communities.
    September 19, 2025 4:33 PM

    Amazon housing fund supports groundbreaking of Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest family shelter and affordable housing in Burien

    Senthil Sankaran (5th from the left) joins Washington State Governor Bob Fergusen (to Senthil’s left), local elected officials, and representatives from Mary’s Place and Mercy Housing at the official groundbreaking.
    With support from Amazon, Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest broke ground today on a project that will combine an emergency family shelter with permanent affordable housing. The campus will provide shelter and comprehensive services for 200 family members alongside 90 units of affordable housing, including 34 units designated for families transitioning from homelessness to stable housing.
    The Burien campus represents a new approach to addressing family homelessness by creating a seamless pathway from crisis to stability. Families will benefit from wraparound services including health resources, educational support, and economic empowerment programs as well as connections to schools and community networks.
    "Amazon has a long history of support for Mary's Place, and we're humbled to support both Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest in this project. Public-private partnerships are essential as we tackle complex challenges, including homelessness," said David Zapolsky, chief global affairs and legal officer at Amazon. "Amazon is proud to support innovative solutions that make a tangible difference in our communities. This project represents a significant step forward in our collective efforts to ensure every family has a safe place to call home."
    Governor Bob Ferguson emphasized the collaborative nature of the initiative: "Housing is too expensive in Washington, and there is not enough of it. For many Washingtonians, this campus will be a path from crisis to stability—and a place to call home."
    When the project is completed in 2027, the campus will stand as a model, here and elsewhere, for how communities can address family homelessness.
    "This project represents a fundamental shift in how we approach family homelessness," said Dominique Alex, chief executive officer of Mary's Place. "By collocating emergency shelter with affordable housing, we're creating a true continuum of care that allows families to rebuild their lives without the disruption of moving to unfamiliar neighborhoods. This model recognizes that stable housing is critical, and it's exactly what families tell us they need most. We are deeply grateful for the tremendous support from so many partners, funders, and advocates who have helped us reach this milestone."

    Amazon delivers more than 60 million meals to families facing food insecurity

    Amazon extended its free home food delivery commitment through 2028 to help millions of families access nutritious food.

    September 12, 2025 9:59 AM

    Celebrating 10,000 affordable homes: Partnerships driving housing impact across Puget Sound

    When Amazon announced our housing commitments in 2021, we set ambitious goals to address the affordable housing crisis in our hometown communities. Today, thanks to collaboration with community organizations, public officials, and mission-aligned developers, we've reached a significant milestone: more than 10,000 affordable homes created or preserved across the Puget Sound region. What makes these investments truly special is our commitment to ensuring these properties remain affordable for 99 years—securing housing stability for generations to come.
    This achievement represents over $900 million in housing investments in the Puget Sound, benefitting 22,000 residents. Behind these numbers are families with access to stable, quality housing near jobs, schools, and transportation.
    "When I think about these affordable homes, I see families finding stability and new opportunities," said Alice Shobe, director of community impact at Amazon. "I'm grateful to celebrate this milestone with our amazing housing partners, passionate community advocates, and the local officials who've championed these efforts. These are solutions that will make a difference for generations to come."
    These 10,000 homes represent meaningful collaborations across the Puget Sound region. From our $100 million commitment to transit-oriented housing in Bellevue, to partnering with BRIDGE Housing to preserve 200 affordable apartments in Kirkland, each project responds to unique local needs. The Four Amigos Beloved Community in Seattle's Columbia City neighborhood offers multi-generational families spacious homes near light rail, with the newly-opened Connection Angle Lake with Mercy Housing Northwest providing 130 energy-efficient homes.
    "Amazon's partnership has been transformative for affordable housing development in the Puget Sound region. Their approach combines critical financial resources with a genuine commitment to long-term community stability through 99-year affordability requirements," said Joe Thompson, president, Mercy Housing Northwest. "At Connection Angle Lake, this partnership allowed us to create not just housing, but a community where residents can thrive near transit, jobs, and services."
    Amazon is energized by what these partnerships have accomplished and are actively seeking additional opportunities to collaborate. Amazon's housing fund is currently accepting applications for Puget Sound projects through September 30.

    Everything you need to know about Amazon’s housing fund—a $3.6 billion commitment to help people access affordable housing

    Amazon is innovating to help keep communities affordable, today and for generations to come.

    August 4, 2025 12:11 PM

    Prime Video, Seattle Seahawks, and Smithsonian team up to spark a love of reading in Seattle youth

    Prime Video, the Seattle Seahawks, and the Smithsonian Institution brought their "TNF for Good" literacy initiative to Boys & Girls Clubs of King County's Ballard location.
    More than 125 kids were helped by Amazon volunteers to choose from a "lawn library" of over 1,300 books that were on display at the event. Those who attended designed bookmarks, engaged with Seattle Seahawks Legend Ben Obomanu, and took photos with Seahawks mascot Blitz.
    The Seattle event represents one stop in the nationwide Thursday Night Football collaboration between Prime Video, Boys & Girls Clubs of America, and the Smithsonian that aims to donate 1 million books to youth across all NFL markets in 2025. The effort reflects the partners' shared commitment to promoting literacy and fostering a love of reading among young people.
    "Having a partner like the Smithsonian curate a collection of books rich in diverse stories and perspectives reflects the vibrancy of our 32 Clubs. At Boys & Girls Clubs, we use these stories to spark a love of reading that fuels imagination, and when a young person's imagination is ignited, it opens the door to a thriving, full life," Boys & Girls Clubs of King County CEO Lori Bae said. "We're especially grateful to the Seattle Seahawks, who go beyond the game by sending their support with legends, dancers, and Blitz to inspire and encourage our youth. And to Amazon, who consistently shows up to ensure every young person has access to the tools, experiences, and support they need to thrive."
    "There's something truly magical about watching a child's face light up when they find a book that speaks to them," said Kevin Burkhart, board member of Boys & Girls Clubs of King County and director of Echo Product & Marketing at Amazon. "Being here today reminds me why this work matters so much. These moments create lasting impact that will extend far beyond today."
    July 28, 2025 1:18 PM

    Amazon and Ascend Hospitality Group support 800 Bellevue students with back-to-school event

    Bellevue Deputy Mayor Mo Malakoutian; Elaina Morris, co-founder and CEO of Ascend Hospitality Group; David Zapolsky, chief global affairs and legal officer at Amazon; and Bellevue Mayor Lynne Robinson
    Photo by Hannah Corbin
    In celebration of the new school year, Amazon and Ascend Hospitality Group (AHG) teamed up for the fifth year of "Ascend Has Your Back," providing backpacks to 800 local students in need. Students enjoyed an afternoon at a "Summer of Soccer" party at Amazon Fresh in Bellevue, where they received backpacks for school, personalized their packs with school supplies and fun keychains, played soccer-influenced games, met members of the Eastside's new pre-professional soccer club Midlakes United, and enjoyed lunch courtesy of Amazon.
    Amazon's Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer David Zapolsky joined Bellevue Mayor Lynne Robinson, Bellevue Deputy Mayor Mo Malakoutian, and AHG Co-Founder and CEO Elaina Morris at the event. Five nonprofit partners—Boys & Girls Club of Bellevue, Rainier Athletes, Bellevue Family YMCA, Bellevue Lifespring, and 4 Tomorrow—also participated alongside dozens of Amazon volunteers to help students prepare for the school year. Amazon Fresh provided hundreds of healthy snacks for students to take home.
    "Education is a huge part of what we care about, and we want to do what we can to ensure access to education for all students in our communities," said David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer at Amazon. "In 2017, we started Amazon Future Engineer, and we've been able to reach more than 2 million students around the world, including students in Bellevue, with everything from elementary school exposure to STEM to post-secondary scholarships. I continue to be inspired by our community's efforts to support students."
    July 25, 2025 3:25 PM

    Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, turns music passion into community action

    Photo by Kari Quaas, Photographer/Photographs by Kari Quaas
    Amazon and Bumbershoot kicked off their second annual Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, campaign at Rainier Beach Urban Farm and Wetlands. Volunteers gathered to harvest crops in support of food security efforts across the Puget Sound and in return, receive a free ticket to Bumbershoot.

    9 ways Amazon employees volunteered during our 2025 Global Month of Volunteering

    Here’s how Amazon employees support organizations and local communities around the world.

    The initiative builds on last year's success, which resulted in 127,000 pounds of food collected for local food banks. This year, the program aims to mobilize more than 2,000 community volunteers to support food banks in delivering 15,000 meals. As a thank you, each volunteer will receive a free two-day festival ticket. It's a powerful incentive to address one of our region's most pressing challenges—one in 10 Washingtonians faces food insecurity.
    "Seattle's arts and music scene is core to this city's identity and an integral part of our cultural fabric," said David Zapolsky, Amazon chief global affairs and legal officer. "We're extending Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, mobilizing 2,000 volunteers and employees to provide 15,000 meals to those in need across 14 food banks in the Puget Sound. This initiative reflects how the arts can be a catalyst for addressing critical needs in our communities, and Amazon's commitment to supporting local traditions and the food security of our neighbors. When we combine Seattle's creative spirit with direct community action, we can transform the joy of music into meaningful impact for those who need it most."
    Since stepping in as a title sponsor in 2023 following a four-year hiatus, Amazon has helped revitalize Bumbershoot, making it more accessible through reduced ticket pricing and providing thousands of free tickets to underserved communities. The festival now welcomes approximately 40,000 attendees over Labor Day weekend, August 30-31. Learn more and sign up to volunteer.
    June 23, 2025 2:04 PM

    Amazon employees beautify Puget Sound communities during Global Month of Volunteering

    As part of Amazon's fourth annual Global Month of Volunteering (GMV), more than 7,600 employees volunteered their time at more than 580 different events across the Puget Sound. From downtown Seattle to Bellevue and places in between, teams of employees worked with more than 220 community organizations to clean up local parks, pack essential supplies, and prepare and serve food.
    In Bellevue, Amazonians participated in the City of Bellevue’s "Keep Bellevue Beautiful" initiative. Volunteers fanned out across downtown Bellevue, collecting trash and debris to enhance the city’s shared spaces.
    “We are grateful for the opportunity to be part of the 'Keep Bellevue Beautiful' initiative,” said Beryl Tomay, VP of Amazon Transportation. “Our volunteers' efforts reflect Amazon's commitment to the communities where we live and work.”
    These park and neighborhood cleanups represent just a fraction of GMV's impact. From joining Backpack Brigade to pack weekend food bags for Seattle students who rely on school meals, to hosting mock interviews and resume building sessions for adults in need of work, employees worldwide volunteered alongside community organizations where support is needed most.
    Amazon’s community engagement extends beyond volunteer hours. We're working side-by-side with community partners to increase access to affordable housing, alleviate hunger, strengthen education, and help those impacted by natural disasters when they occur. Since 2021, Amazon has donated more than $244 million to local charities and nonprofit organizations across the region, while investing $720 million through Amazon's housing fund to preserve and create over 9,900 affordable homes throughout Puget Sound.
    June 17, 2025 1:59 PM

    AWS, Seattle Seahawks, and Play Like a Girl' team up to inspire next generation of STEM leaders

    2025 AWS Tech TouchdownPhoto by KAYSHAWN MOUNARATH
    When 100 middle and high school girls from across the Seattle area arrived at the Virginia Mason Athletic Center this month, they found more than just the Seahawks' practice facility–they discovered a world where sports and technology seamlessly intersect.
    Tech Touchdown, a collaboration between Amazon Web Services (AWS), the Seattle Seahawks, and nonprofit Play Like a Girl, transformed the Seahawks training center in Renton into an interactive learning lab where young athletes explored how cloud technology powers modern sports experiences.
    "It was an awesome and inspiring day at the Seahawks Training Facility," said Julia White, AWS chief marketing officer. "Helping these talented young women see themselves in both sports and STEM was incredibly rewarding.”
    2025 AWS Tech TouchdownPhoto by KAYSHAWN MOUNARATH
    Participants rotated through three AWS-powered stations. At My Cause My Cleats, students used generative AI to design custom cleats similar to those NFL players wear for charitable causes, each taking home a custom design keepsake. The NFL Football Throw station tested quarterback abilities with sensor-equipped footballs that feature the same technology used to track throws by NFL players. Finally, at a Seahawks Hologram station, participants could “interact” with former Seahawks wide receiver Ben Obomanu and see themselves transformed into holograms.
    The day also featured appearances by current Seahawks’ players, including wide receiver Dareke Young and linebacker Boye Mafe who participated in flag football drills alongside students.
    "We're going to show him how it's done!" one student declared before showcasing her athleticism and taking the flag.

    Amazon plans to invest $20 billion in Pennsylvania to expand cloud computing infrastructure and advance AI innovation

    Planned investment will establish multiple innovation campuses, create 1,250 jobs, and support new workforce development training programs and local community projects

    May 21, 2025 4:51 PM

    Breaking Ground on Acer House: Amazon-backed project brings bold vision to Seattle’s Central District

    David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs & Legal Officer at Amazon, joined Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell and local housing developers Great Expectations and Urban Black, to break ground on Acer House, a 121-unit mixed-use community that will provide affordable homes and micro-retail spaces for local entrepreneurs in Seattle’s Central District.
    Made possible through a $12.6 million low-rate loan from Amazon's housing fund, the development represents an innovative approach to creating affordable housing without traditional funding mechanisms like tax credits or subordinate public loans. Acer House will serve households earning between 40% and 80% of Area Median Income (AMI), and feature six commercial spaces for small local businesses.
    "By combining affordable housing with opportunities for local entrepreneurs, this project helps preserve the cultural fabric of the Central District while creating pathways for economic mobility," said David Zapolsky. "We're honored that Amazon's housing fund could help make this vision a reality.”
    The project leverages the City of Seattle's Multi-Family Tax Exemption program and fulfills the City’s Mandatory Housing Affordability requirements. What makes Amazon's housing investment particularly impactful is that while many public funds specify affordability for 15 to 40 years, Amazon's housing fund ensures affordability for 99 years on most developments.
    Acer House is a recent example of Amazon's commitment to affordable housing. Since launching with an initial $2 billion commitment in 2021, Amazon has pledged $3.6 billion toward creating or preserving 35,000 affordable homes across the Puget Sound, National Capital, and Nashville regions. In the Puget Sound region alone, Amazon has committed $780 million to support more than 9,900 affordable homes, with $230 million dedicated specifically to Seattle.
    May 15, 2025 11:41 AM

    Amazon contributes $250,000 to the Chinatown International District to establish Ambassadors Program, fostering a safe and vibrant neighborhood

    Mayor Harrell announces a combined $1 million investment by the City, The Asian American Foundation (TAAF), and Amazon to improve “blocks, businesses, and lives” in the heart of the historic neighborhood.
    In a shared effort to support safety, economic revitalization, and community well-being in one of Seattle’s most historic and culturally rich neighborhoods, Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell, TAAF, Amazon, and the Chinatown International District Business Improvement Area (CIDBIA) announced the Chinatown-International District (CID) Ambassadors Program, launching June 1, 2025.
    The CID Ambassadors Program will train neighborhood ambassadors to serve Japantown, Chinatown, and Little Saigon. These teams will work block by block to assist small businesses, welcome visitors, and foster safer, more vibrant neighborhoods.
    "The Chinatown-International District is a vital part of Seattle's cultural and economic fabric and Amazon is proud to support the CID Ambassadors Program as part of our contributions to the revitalization of this important neighborhood,” said David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer of Amazon. “Our investments in Friends of Little Sài Gòn and to revive the CID Night Market reinforce our commitment to fostering economic development, public safety, and cultural celebration.”
    This also marks the launch of TAAF’s new “TAAF Cities” initiative to spur historic public and private investment to strengthen cities with large Asian American and Pacific Islander (AAPI) populations, of which Amazon is a founding partner. “TAAF Cities” bring together local community, government, business, and nonprofit leaders to develop innovative, community-rooted solutions that promote economic opportunity, safety, and cultural pride. Seattle, a major cultural and economic hub with some of the nation’s oldest and most dynamic AAPI communities, was chosen to kickstart the effort.”

    5 ways Amazon is giving back to communities across the US

    We work with thousands of partners to address a range of critical social needs in our hometown communities and across the U.S.

    May 8, 2025 6:49 AM

    Amazon's Josh Hirschland named to Puget Sound Business Journal's 40 Under 40

    Puget Sound Business Journal named Josh Hirschland, principal product manager for Amazon Community Impact, a 40 Under 40 honoree for his innovative work addressing food insecurity in the Puget Sound community.
    As the Amazon Community Delivery program lead, Hirschland applies his logistics expertise to create sustainable, scalable solutions that help get meals directly to the doorsteps of community members in need. Under his guidance, Amazon has offered pro bono delivery for more than 40 million meals to people experiencing food insecurity since 2020—2 million meals across the Puget Sound region.
    The program launched during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic when food pantries faced unprecedented demand and many families couldn't leave their homes. Hirschland leveraged Amazon's last-mile delivery network to bridge this gap, transforming an initial pilot into a fully operational program within weeks.
    Today, Amazon Community Delivery partners with more than 35 organizations in 16 states and expanded to the UK last year. In the Puget Sound region, Amazon collaborates with seven different food banks, including United Way of King County, to serve more than 4,000 families.
    Amazon believes everyone should have access to healthy food and continues to address the rising rates of food insecurity by partnering with local organizations.
    Learn more about how Amazon delivers meals to families in need across Puget Sound.

    How Amazon helps data center communities thrive

    Amazon’s investment in its data center locations is helping create jobs, support economic growth, provide education and training opportunities, and much more.

    May 5, 2025 1:37 PM

    Amazon is helping fund 9,900+ affordable homes across the Puget Sound

    Jared Johnson of Chinatown Preservation & Development talks with Seattleites about Beacon Pacific Village, an affordable housing community with a childcare center and healthy aging and wellness center, situated close to transit, schools, healthcare, and shopping. This multi-generational community received a $3M grant from Amazon’s housing fund, which has fund or preserve more than 9,900 affordable homes across the Puget Sound.
    Watch the video to learn more.
    May 1, 2025 1:44 PM

    Amazon and the Seattle Storm engage Puget Sound youth through Community Hoops clinics

    Amazon's continued partnership with the Seattle Storm is supporting the second annual series of Community Hoops clinics—a collaborative effort to bring free, accessible basketball programming to youth across the Puget Sound region.
    As part of the collaboration, 45 youth participants from Mary’s Place participated in a clinic at the Storm’s Center for Basketball Performance in Seattle’s Interbay neighborhood—many of them experiencing the sport for the first time. A five-week series at the Family First Community Center in Renton welcomed 50 youth weekly from the Benson Hill neighborhood. In Tukwila, 88 students from elementary to high school levels came together for a day of skills-building and community.
    “The kids had a wonderful time and were so grateful for the experience,” said Zoë Bill, Youth Services Director at Mary’s Place. “Beyond enjoying basketball, they walked away feeling celebrated and a part of something special. Opportunities like this have a lasting impact.”
    The Seattle Storm’s youth basketball program, Jr. Storm, leads the clinics—putting together drills, offering encouragement, and sharing their love for the game. Their involvement helps create an inspiring environment where kids not only learn basketball fundamentals but also connect with role models.
    In 2024, Amazon and the Storm hosted eight clinics in partnership with local organizations—reaching hundreds of young athletes and their families. These clinics are just one example of the many ways Amazon invests in local communities across the Puget Sound. We remain committed to supporting neighborhoods where our employees and customers live and work through collaboration with local organizations and programs that create meaningful opportunities for future generations.

    Amazon data center communities: Here’s what’s happening near data centers across the US

    Get the latest updates on how Amazon supports small businesses, creates jobs, sets up sustainability initiatives, and develops educational programs near AWS data centers.

    April 22, 2025 9:42 AM

    Amazon gives $2 million to support critical food security initiatives in Seattle region

    Amazon's investment will help two local food banks expand their capacity to serve community members with essential groceries and services.
    Amazon provided $2 million to support the growth of two food banks in the Seattle region, with grants of $1 million each to White Center Food Bank and Rainier Valley Food Bank. The donations will help both organizations expand their capacity to provide more community members experiencing food insecurity with vital groceries.
    White Center Food Bank will use Amazon's grant to support its move to their new, permanent facility in downtown White Center. The location provides improved accessibility through nearby public transportation routes and allows the organization to expand its services.
    Rainier Valley Food Bank will use its grant to support the renovation and expansion of its facility. The improvements will create a community food hub with that can meet urgent food needs with increased capacity for food storage, a commercial kitchen, and gathering spaces to build community.
    In addition to supporting the critical work of local food banks, Amazon is committed to addressing food insecurity through multiple initiatives across the communities where we operate. We collaborate with nonprofit organizations, leverage our logistics expertise, and engage our employees to help provide millions of meals to individuals and families in need. Since 2020, our Community Delivery program, which provides free delivery of charitable groceries, has delivered more than 40 million meals with 35 partners in 16 states.
    Learn more about Amazon's commitment to food security and how we’re helping fight hunger.

    Amazon commits over $70 million in scholarships to students across the US since 2019

    Amazon has awarded 200 high school seniors with scholarships of up to $40,000 each, plus a paid internship opportunity at Amazon.

    February 27, 2025 4:49 PM

    Amazon's $100 million commitment to affordable housing in the City of Bellevue

    Amazon is committing $100 million in the City of Bellevue to accelerate the production of affordable housing for low-to-moderate income families. This commitment will support projects throughout Bellevue, with a particular focus on projects supported by the City and on Bellevue's three city-owned transit-oriented sites—giving low-to-moderate income families close proximity to community resources.
    David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer at Amazon, announced the news at the ground breaking of Spring District, a BRIDGE Housing Community that will bring 234 affordable homes to the City of Bellevue through a transit-oriented development.
    Learn more about Spring District and Amazon's $100 million commitment to the City of Bellevue.

    Everything you need to know about Amazon’s housing fund—a $3.6 billion commitment to help people access affordable housing

    Amazon is innovating to help keep communities affordable, today and for generations to come.

    February 26, 2025 1:20 PM

    Amazon’s return to office boosts Seattle’s foot traffic and local economy

    Amazon teams returning to offices are playing a major role in the continued revitalization of downtown Seattle. New data is showing a surge in foot traffic—benefiting local businesses and the broader downtown economy.
    The Downtown Seattle Association (DSA) reports that weekday worker foot traffic in South Lake Union and the Denny Triangle reached 74% of January 2019 levels, up from 62% the previous year. Across downtown, foot traffic increased 9% from January 2024, reaching 57% of pre-pandemic levels. Nearly 2 million unique visitors came downtown in January, 14% more than the previous year.

    16 cool things we found inside the Spheres, Amazon’s urban rainforest in downtown Seattle

    Take a look inside the Amazon Spheres as we celebrate their fifth anniversary at Amazon’s Puget Sound headquarters.

    Amazon’s presence has been a key driver of this uptick. Our 50,000 Seattle employees, along with those in Bellevue and Redmond, are bringing a sense of renewed vibrancy to these communities. Amazon teams are also feeling energized by the shift—excited by the innovation, collaboration, and connection that takes place with teams collaborating in-person.
    Many local business owners are noticing a welcome increase in weekday customers. “Coffee shops are full, lunch spots are buzzing, and there’s more activity in the area,” noted Downtown Seattle Association President and CEO Jon Scholes.
    You can read more about Amazon’s return to office plans as we continue to spark energy and innovation on behalf of Amazon’s customers here.
    February 3, 2025 10:44 AM

    Amazon’s commitment to flexible, sustainable commutes in HQ1

    At Amazon, we value the connections and opportunities that come from being part of vibrant, downtown communities. By investing in innovative transit options and fostering partnerships, we are able to offer employees a variety of commuter benefits and services to reach their destinations.
    Across our headquarters cities and in other major metro areas, our employees have access to a variety of reimbursable public transportation costs, free commuter shuttles, and flexible monthly subsidies for commuting expenses.
    In Puget Sound, we take additional steps to enhance connectivity and help reduce traffic congestion, including:
    • Expanded shuttle services: We increased shuttle routes serving our Puget Sound HQ, including over 30 commuter shuttle routes as far as Mill Creek, North Bend, and West Seattle, intercampus shuttles between our Seattle, Bellevue, and Redmond offices, and shuttles that transport Amazonians to the office from transit hubs and locations around the Puget Sound region.
    • Rideshare: Amazon recently launched a first of its kind Lyft Pass program in Seattle and Bellevue, allowing employees to utilize their Flexible Commuter Subsidy to match rides with fellow Amazonians.
    • Biking: Through their Flexible Commuter Subsidy, Amazon employees can utilize the subsidy to cover costs including: bike leases, bike-share, maintenance, and bike parking. Offices also include bike cages for storage and showers for bikers to get ready for the work day.
    • Sustainable transportation partnerships: Amazon is proud to support local partners that share a commitment to greener commuting options. BellHop, the first all-electric shuttle service in the Pacific Northwest, is one partnership that helps employees replace car trips with a sustainable micro-transit option.
    • Investing in public infrastructure: We work with policymakers and support transit investments that help expand access to public transportation.
      • Amazon advocated for the Move Ahead Washington statewide transportation package which funds over $730 million in traffic reduction projects, including free transit for residents age 18 or younger.
      • Amazon has contributed more than $1.6 million to campaigns that have expanded transit access and invested in and preserved transportation infrastructure.
      • Amazon directly invests in transportation infrastructure, including $10.8 million toward Eastrail and the Grand Connection Crossing.
    As we move forward, Amazon will continue to explore new ways to enhance our employees’ commute experiences while helping build stronger, greener communities.
    January 30, 2025 12:57 PM

    Amazon supports Memorial Stadium redevelopment in Seattle

    Amazon is proudly contributing $2 million to the redevelopment of Memorial Stadium, helping to ensure that the iconic venue remains a cherished part of the Seattle community and a vital part of its future. This donation clinched the project’s fundraising by helping it meet its final goal, which included $110 million in public and private funding.
    Originally built in 1947 to honor Seattle students who lost their lives in World War II, Memorial Stadium has served as a meaningful community hub for decades. From high school football games and graduations, to large-scale events like the Seattle World’s Fair ceremonies, the stadium has played an integral role in the cultural fabric of the city. As the venue has fallen into disrepair, Seattle Public Schools and the City of Seattle partnered with One Roof Stadium Partnership (ORSP) for an ambitious redesign that will result in a modern, state-of-the-art facility. The redevelopment will allow Seattle Public Schools to retain ownership and primary use, while also increasing its capacity and capabilities to host even more events.
    “Amazon’s donation to the Memorial Stadium demonstrates our long-term commitment to building accessible communities in the Puget Sound,” said David Zapolsky, Senior Vice President, Global Public Policy and General Counsel at Amazon. “We’re thrilled to help transform the stadium into a space for everyone in the community, where neighbors can come together and connect. This reimagined stadium will open doors for more community members and expand upon its legacy, remaining a cherished part of Seattle’s history for years to come.”
    "Amazon's generous contribution to the Memorial Stadium redevelopment showcases the power of public-private collaboration in driving systemic change,” said Tracy Myers, Executive Director of King County Play Equity Coalition. "By modernizing this iconic venue, we're not only preserving Seattle's heritage but also creating a space where young athletes from all backgrounds, particularly those from historically underserved communities, can access the transformative benefits of sports and physical activity.”
    Scheduled for completion by 2027, the new stadium will increase capacity to over 8,000 and feature additional spaces for athletics, arts, and community events. It will honor the original Memorial Wall, with new elements aligning with the City of Seattle’s goals for equity, sustainability, and community resilience. The reimagined stadium will remain an important centerpiece of Seattle Center—continuing its legacy of creating opportunities for students and the broader community to thrive for years to come.
    The donation is also part of Amazon’s broader commitment to the Puget Sound region, where we continue to invest in housing, education, food security, and other causes that make a tangible impact in our community. Last year alone, we contributed $68 million to 120 Washington state community partners. Since 2021, we have also committed more than $780 million to help create or preserve affordable housing for nearly 19,000 people through the Amazon Housing Equity Fund. Read more about our commitment to the Puget Sound community in our 2024 Community Impact Report.
    December 19, 2024 6:10 AM

    Amazon invests in vital program to combat food insecurity for Tukwila Schools

    Amazon has reaffirmed its dedication to supporting communities across the Puget Sound with an investment in Still Waters, a nonprofit organization addressing food insecurity in the Tukwila area. With an $80,000 commitment, Amazon is ensuring that Still Waters’ after-school and weekend food program remains fully funded through the 2026-2027 school year.
    This vital program currently supports three local elementary schools, reaching 220 students each week. It provides the nutritious meals students need to stay focused, learn effectively, and maintain their overall health. An initial $40,000 donation from Amazon has already secured funding for the 2024-2025 school year, while the remaining $40,000 will co-fund the program’s continuation for the following two school years in collaboration with the City of Tukwila.
    Local leaders have recognized the importance of this partnership. Tukwila Mayor Thomas McLeod expressed his appreciation, stating, “Still Waters provides critical food security for kids in the Tukwila School District. Amazon’s support ensures that kids will have healthy meals and not go hungry.”
    For Still Waters, Amazon’s commitment means the continuation of a program that many families rely on. Abigail Tabason, Executive Director of Still Waters, shared her gratitude, saying, “At Still Waters, our team believes in order to achieve food and educational equity in Tukwila Schools, all students deserve access to nutrition. We are so grateful for the support from Amazon and the City of Tukwila. This will ensure there is no interruption of this program that many Tukwila families rely on.”
    The impact of Amazon’s contributions extends well beyond this latest effort. Since 2021, the company has donated over 8 million pounds of food in Washington State and provided more than $2.9 million to local organizations in the Puget Sound region. These investments are part of Amazon’s broader commitment to addressing food insecurity and supporting individuals and families in need.
    “Amazon has been deeply connected to the Puget Sound region for more than 30 years,” said Taylor Hoang, Senior Manager for Community Engagement at Amazon. “We remain committed to taking a hands-on approach to work with nonprofits and community partners to find creative solutions for some of our region’s most pressing needs. We’re proud to be able to support organizations like Still Waters in their critical work to help combat food insecurity.”
    December 17, 2024 10:41 AM

    Amazon’s Winter Wonderland brings holiday cheer to Puget Sound families

    On Saturday, December 7, Amazon hosted its second annual Winter Wonderland, an event designed to bring holiday joy to underserved children and families in the Puget Sound region.
    Over 1,000 children and their families gathered at the Amazon Meeting Center in Seattle for a day filled with holiday magic. Each child had the chance to select toys, books, and other goodies while creating special memories with their families. Parents and guardians also received a $50 gift credit per child to shop on Amazon.com, with purchases delivered directly to their homes—making the holidays brighter and more convenient.

    01 / 04

    Winter Wonderland welcomed families through partnerships with local nonprofits including Byrd Barr Place, El Centro de la Raza, Treehouse, Rainier Athletes, and Wellspring Family Services. Families also enjoyed photo opportunities, seasonal crafts, refreshments, and live holiday music. Adding to the excitement, former Seattle Seahawks players along with Seahawks mascots and dancers, made special appearances throughout the day.
    Amazon’s Senior Vice President of Global Public Policy, David Zapolsky, had the opportunity to sit down with KING 5’s New Day Northwest to chat about Winter Wonderland while reflecting on how Amazon has cultivated a "culture of giving.” This comes to life through hundreds of thousands of volunteer hours from our teams, and investments that uplift communities where our customers and employees live and work.
    At Winter Wonderland, 300 Amazon volunteers staffed the toy shop, assisted at online shopping stations, led craft activities, and even dressed up as holiday mascots to spread festive cheer.
    November 22, 2024 9:55 AM

    Amazon expands partnerships with Puget Sound food banks to deliver groceries to neighbors in need

    November 15, 2024 9:38 AM

    Amazon and the Gary Sinise Foundation team up to support Washington state first responders

    In honor of National First Responders Day, observed on October 28th, Amazon once again joined forces with the Gary Sinise Foundation (GSF)—a leader in supporting first responders, veterans, and their families—to celebrate and support those who put their lives on the line for their communities.
    Amazon and GSF donated more than $200,000 in new equipment to fire departments across Washington state. This equipment is enhancing first responders’ ability to perform their lifesaving work safely and effectively.
    Amazon and GSF visited local fire departments to present the grants and serve meals to first responder teams as a small token of gratitude. Spokane County Fire District 10, Franklin County Fire District 3, Everett Fire Department and West Pierce Fire & Rescue were all chosen as this partnership prioritizes under-resourced fire departments with larger numbers of volunteer firefighters.
